title: Exponent Calculator - Raise Base to a Power
shortDescription: Raise any base to an exponent (power) and get the result instantly.
description: The exponent calculator raises a base to an exponent (power) using the formula result = base^exponent. It supports negative and decimal exponents, making it useful for math homework, science, and everyday calculations.
metaDescription: Free exponent calculator that raises a base to any power, including negative and decimal exponents. Get accurate results instantly for math and science.

result.label: Result
result.empty: Enter a base and an exponent to see the result.

sections.heading: What is exponentiation?
paragraphs: Exponentiation raises a base to an exponent (power) and is written base^exponent. For a positive whole-number exponent, it means multiplying the base by itself that many times.
For example, 2^3 equals 2 x 2 x 2 = 8. The base is the number being multiplied, and the exponent tells you how many times to use it.

sections.heading: Negative and decimal exponents
paragraphs: A negative exponent gives the reciprocal: 2^-2 = 1 / (2^2) = 0.25. A decimal exponent represents a root: 9^0.5 is the square root of 9, which is 3.
This calculator handles all of these cases, so you can explore powers, reciprocals, and roots without switching tools.

howTo.name: Enter the base
howTo.text: Type the base value, the number you want to raise to a power.

howTo.name: Enter the exponent
howTo.text: Type the exponent (power). You can use negative or decimal values.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The calculator instantly shows base^exponent as the result.

faq.a: It means raising the base to the given power. For a whole exponent, multiply the base by itself that many times, so 3^4 = 3 x 3 x 3 x 3 = 81.
Yes. A negative exponent returns the reciprocal of the positive power, so 2^-3 = 1 / (2^3) = 0.125.

faq.q: What does a decimal exponent do?
faq.a: A decimal exponent represents a root. For example, 16^0.5 is the square root of 16, which equals 4.

faq.q: What is any number raised to the power of 0?
faq.a: Any nonzero number raised to the power of 0 equals 1. The case 0^0 is generally treated as 1 in most calculators.
Yes for whole-number exponents. A negative base with a fractional exponent may not give a real number, so the result can be undefined.
